key_insight: Strain can selectively reprogram chemical ordering in nanoalloys, but the effect depends strongly on particle structure: icosahedra respond much more than truncated octahedra.
engineering_relevance: This suggests substrate-induced strain can be used as a design lever to tune surface composition and catalytic/functional behavior of supported bimetallic nanoparticles.
method: Atomistic molecular dynamics and Monte Carlo simulations
NiPt nanoalloys with truncated octahedral and icosahedral motifs
Controlled tensile and compressive strain applied via an implicit anchored interface
evidence_signal: Simulation results show robust ordering in truncated octahedra under large strain, while icosahedral particles exhibit clear strain-driven Ni surface enrichment under tension.
watch_item: The conclusions are based on an implicit interface model and a specific NiPt system, so transferability to real substrates and other alloy chemistries should be validated.
paper_title: Structure-Dependent Chemical Order Modification in Strained Alloy Nanoparticles
